title: p-Block, d-Block and f-Block Elements
subject: Chemistry
content:
# p-Block, d-Block and f-Block Elements

## Quick Synopsis

Block chemistry is trend-based plus facts about important compounds. Focus on oxidation states, anomalous behaviour, colours, magnetic properties, and reactions of key compounds.

## Must Know

- p-block elements show inert pair effect down heavier groups.
- Second-period elements often behave anomalously due to small size and no d orbitals.
- Transition metals show variable oxidation states, coloured ions, complex formation, and catalytic activity.
- Lanthanide contraction affects size and chemistry of d-block elements after lanthanoids.
- Oxidising/reducing behaviour changes with oxidation state stability.
- Important compounds of nitrogen, oxygen, halogens, sulphur, and transition metals are frequently tested.

## Formulas / Rules

- Magnetic moment: μ = sqrt(n(n+2)) BM for spin-only cases.
- Oxidation state sum equals total charge.
- Disproportionation: same element undergoes oxidation and reduction simultaneously.

## JEE Focus

- Memorize trends for hydrides, oxides, halides, acidic/basic character.
- Know KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 reactions in acidic/basic/neutral media.
- Compare stability of oxidation states across transition series.
- Understand colour from d-d transitions and charge transfer.

## Common Traps

- Not all d-block elements are transition elements in every oxidation state.
- Inert pair effect increases down group 13-16.
- F has only -1 oxidation state in compounds.
- Lanthanides commonly show +3 oxidation state.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you rank acidic/basic character of oxides?
- Can you write key oxyacid trends?
- Can you balance KMnO4/K2Cr2O7 redox reactions?
- Can you explain colour and magnetism of transition ions?
